The key watch-out is that ALL('Date') only removes filters on the Date table - if your model has date columns sitting directly on a fact table (rather than a proper Date dimension with relationships), those columns won't be cleared and the measure will silently return wrong results.

dax: Category Gross Margin Share = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ) - SUM ( Sales[TotalCost] ), ALL ( Product[Category] ) ) )
explanation: This measure calculates each product category's gross margin as a percentage of the total gross margin across all categories - essentially, "what slice of overall profit does this category own?" It computes the current context's gross margin (sales minus cost) in the numerator, then uses ALL(Product[Category]) in the denominator to strip away any category filter and produce the grand-total gross margin, with DIVIDE handling any division-by-zero gracefully. The key watch-out is that ALL(Product[Category]) only removes filters on that single column, so other active filters - such as slicers on region, date, or other product attributes - still apply to the denominator; if you want a true share against a completely unfiltered gross margin, you'd need ALL(Product) or ALL(Sales) depending on your model structure.

dax: Gross Margin % = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales] - [Total Cost], [Total Sales] )
explanation: This measure calculates the gross margin percentage - the share of revenue remaining after deducting direct costs, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.35 for 35%). It simply subtracts the [Total Cost] measure from [Total Sales], divides by [Total Sales], and inherits whatever filter context is active at evaluation time (slice by product, region, date, etc.), so it dynamically reflects the margin for whatever subset of data is in scope. DIVIDE is used instead of the / operator specifically to return BLANK rather than an error when [Total Sales] is zero, which keeps visuals clean. The key watch-out is that this measure is only as reliable as its two dependencies - if [Total Sales] or [Total Cost] have mismatched filter contexts (for example, one ignores a slicer the other respects), the resulting percentage will be silently wrong, so always validate both base measures behave consistently under the same filters.

dax: Percentile Rank Customer by Sales = DIVIDE ( RANKX ( ALL ( Customer[CustomerName] ), [Total Sales], , ASC ), COUNTROWS ( ALL ( Customer[CustomerName] ) ) )
explanation:
This measure expresses each customer's sales rank as a percentile, telling you what fraction of all customers that customer outsells - a value of 0.95 means the customer outperforms 95% of the customer base. It works by first using RANKX over the complete customer list (ALL removes any filter context on CustomerName) to assign an ascending rank based on Total Sales, then divides that rank by the total count of customers to normalise it to a 0-1 scale. The ASC order means the lowest-selling customer gets rank 1 and the highest gets the top rank, so higher percentile = better performer, which is the intuitive reading but worth confirming against your stakeholders' expectations.

The key watch-out is tie handling: RANKX defaults to Dense ranking when no ties argument is specified, so if multiple customers share the same Total Sales value they all receive the same rank, and nobody gets the skipped ranks - this can cause several customers to land on an identical percentile and the maximum percentile to fall short of 1.0 if there are ties at the top.

dax: Total Extended Cost = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitCost] )
explanation: This measure calculates the total cost of goods sold by multiplying quantity by unit cost at the row level and summing the results across all sales transactions - essentially the extended (line-level) cost rolled up to whatever grain the visual or filter context demands. It uses SUMX to iterate row by row through the Sales table, performing the multiplication before aggregating, which ensures the math happens at the correct granularity rather than multiplying pre-aggregated totals. The result respects any active filter context, so it will correctly slice by product, date, region, or any other dimension without additional configuration. The key watch-out is that this measure assumes both Quantity and UnitCost are stored at the transaction row level in the Sales table; if UnitCost is resolved via a relationship to a separate table (e.g., a Products table), you'd need to reference that column explicitly, otherwise you risk blank or incorrect values if the column doesn't physically exist in Sales.

dax: Total Subcategories = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Subcategory] )
explanation: This measure counts the number of unique product subcategories visible in the current filter context. It works by scanning the Subcategory column in the Product table and returning the count of distinct values after all active slicers, filters, and visual context have been applied. No iteration or time intelligence is involved - it's a straightforward aggregation that respects whatever filters are in play. The key watch-out is that if Subcategory contains blanks, DISTINCTCOUNT will include the blank as one distinct value, which can inflate your count by one and mislead users who assume every counted subcategory is a named, legitimate category.

dax: Min Quantity Per Order = MINX ( VALUES ( Sales[OrderNumber] ), CALCULATE ( SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) ) )
explanation: This measure finds the smallest total quantity across all individual orders in the current filter context - useful for understanding the lightest order a customer, product, or period has placed. It works by first using VALUES to produce a one-column table of distinct order numbers, then iterating over each one with MINX; inside each iteration, CALCULATE implicitly inherits the row context transition, so SUM returns the total quantity for that single order, and MINX keeps the lowest value seen. The critical watch-out is that VALUES respects the existing filter context, so if your model has incomplete relationships or an order number appears in multiple fact rows spanning different filter combinations, the per-order sum may silently aggregate more rows than you expect - always verify that OrderNumber genuinely identifies a single transaction in your data.

dax: High Quantity Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], Sales[Quantity] > 100 )
explanation: This measure calculates total sales revenue restricted to only those transactions where the quantity sold exceeds 100 units, effectively isolating high-volume line items from the broader sales figure. It works by wrapping the base [Total Sales] measure inside CALCULATE, which adds a row-level filter on Sales[Quantity] > 100 - this filter is applied directly to the Sales table and stacks with (or can be overridden by) any existing filter context from slicers, rows, or columns in the report. The result is the sum of sales only for rows meeting that quantity threshold, with all other report filters still in play. The key watch-out is that this filter targets individual rows in the Sales table, so if [Total Sales] is built on a different granularity or references a related table, the filter may not propagate as expected - and if your data rarely or never has quantities above 100, the measure will silently return blank rather than flagging a data or threshold issue.

dax: Total Products = DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[ProductKey] )
explanation: Counts the number of unique products that exist in the current filter context - for example, how many distinct products were sold in a selected category, region, or time period, depending on what slicers or report filters are active. It works by scanning the ProductKey column of the Product table and returning the count of non-duplicate, non-blank values after all active filters have been applied. The key watch-out is that this measure counts products visible in the filter context, so if ProductKey appears in a fact table column rather than the dimension, blank or missing keys from unmatched rows can silently inflate or deflate the count - always anchor DISTINCTCOUNT to the clean dimension table column, not a foreign key in a fact table.

dax: Profit per Customer = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[Profit] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Sales[CustomerKey] ) )
explanation:
This measure calculates the average profit generated per unique customer, giving a sense of how much value each customer contributes on average. It sums all profit values in the current filter context, then divides by the count of distinct customer keys within that same context - so it naturally slices correctly by region, product, time period, or any other slicer on your report. The division is wrapped in DIVIDE rather than the / operator, which silently returns blank instead of a hard error when there are no customers.

The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on CustomerKey values present in the Sales table, not in a separate Customers dimension - so any customer who exists in your customer master but has zero sales in the current filter window is excluded from the denominator, which will inflate the metric compared to a definition that counts all active or registered customers.

dax: Total Refund Amount = SUM ( Sales[RefundAmount] )
explanation: This measure sums every value in the RefundAmount column of the Sales table to give the total dollar value of refunds within whatever filter context is active - a visual, slicer, or row in a matrix will automatically slice it down to the relevant subset. It's a straightforward aggregation with no explicit filter manipulation, so it respects all context inherited from the report canvas. The key watch-out is that it assumes RefundAmount stores refunds as positive numbers; if your source data records them as negative values (common in transactional systems that net credits against sales), this measure will return a negative total, and you may need to wrap it in ABS() or adjust upstream - otherwise it can silently misrepresent refund magnitude in visuals that expect positive figures.

dax: Revenue MoM Change = [Total Revenue] - [Revenue Prior Month]
explanation: This measure calculates the absolute month-over-month change in revenue by subtracting the prior month's revenue from the current month's revenue, giving analysts a simple pound/dollar delta to track growth or decline. It relies entirely on whatever filter context is active - typically a month selection on a date table - and delegates the time-shift logic to the [Revenue Prior Month] measure, which almost certainly uses DATEADD or PREVIOUSMONTH to shift the context back one calendar month. The key watch-out is that if the date table has gaps, the current period is the very first month in your data, or [Revenue Prior Month] returns BLANK, the result will equal the full current-month revenue rather than BLANK or zero, which can silently distort period-over-period trend visuals and percentage calculations built on top of this measure.

dax: Country Rank by Sales = RANKX ( ALL ( 'Geography'[Country] ), [Total Sales], , DESC )
explanation: This measure ranks each country from highest to lowest based on Total Sales, giving rank 1 to the top-selling country. It works by first using ALL to strip any existing filter on the Country column, then iterating over every country in that column and evaluating Total Sales for each one - RANKX then positions the current country relative to that full set in descending order. The critical watch-out is that ALL only removes filters on Geography[Country]; if your report has other filters active (say, a year slicer or a product category filter), those filters remain in context and will affect how Total Sales is calculated for each country, meaning the rank reflects "top country under the current filter conditions" rather than an unconditional global rank - which may or may not be what you intend.

dax: Transactions YoY Change = [Total Transactions] - [Transactions PY]
explanation: This measure calculates the year-over-year absolute change in transaction count - the raw difference between the current period's transactions and the same period one year prior. It works by subtracting a prior-year measure (which internally uses SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR or a similar time-intelligence function to shift the date filter back 12 months) from the current [Total Transactions], both evaluated within whatever filter context exists on the report. The result is a simple delta: positive means growth, negative means decline. The key watch-out is that if [Transactions PY] returns BLANK for periods where no prior-year data exists - such as the first year in your date table or for dates beyond the last actual transaction - the subtraction will silently treat BLANK as zero, which can produce misleading results; always validate that your date table fully covers the comparison window and consider wrapping the measure in an ISBLANK check if partial periods are a concern.

dax: Revenue YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Revenue] - [Revenue PY], [Revenue PY] )
explanation: This measure shows year-over-year growth as a percentage - how much bigger or smaller this period's revenue is compared with the same stretch a year ago. It subtracts last year's figure ([Revenue PY]) from this year's and divides the gap by last year's number to turn it into a percentage; the [Revenue PY] piece is itself a time-intelligence measure that shifts the dates back twelve months (usually with SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR), and DIVIDE keeps things tidy by returning blank if last year's value is zero. So a result of 0.15 means '15% up on last year.' The key watch-out is that the calculation inherits whatever dates are on the visual, so if the current period isn't finished - say you're partway through this month - you'll be comparing a half-finished period against a complete one a year ago, which makes growth look far worse than it really is.

dax: Monthly Sales Rank = RANKX ( ALL ( 'Date'[YearMonth] ), [Total Sales], , DESC, DENSE )
explanation: This measure ranks each calendar month by its Total Sales figure, telling you whether a given month was the 1st, 2nd, or 3rd highest-selling month (and so on) across the entire date range. It works by removing all filters on the YearMonth column with ALL, then iterating over every distinct month in that column and evaluating Total Sales for each one, finally assigning a dense descending rank so that tied months share the same rank and no numbers are skipped. The key watch-out is that ALL only strips filters from 'Date'[YearMonth] - any external filter on another column, such as a product category or region slicer, will still apply when Total Sales is evaluated for each month, meaning the rank reflects "best month for this filtered subset" rather than an absolute global rank; if you want a truly unconditional rank, you'd need ALLSELECTED or a broader ALL('Date') depending on your intent.

dax: Discount per Unit = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[DiscountAmount] ), SUM ( Sales[Quantity] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average discount given per unit sold - essentially how much discount, in currency, is being applied for every unit across the current filter context (selected product, region, time period, etc.). It works by summing all discount amounts and all quantities within whatever filter context is active, then safely dividing the two totals using DIVIDE, which returns BLANK rather than an error if the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that this is a ratio of sums, not an average of per-row ratios - meaning it weights heavily toward high-volume rows, so if you have a mix of high-quantity/low-discount and low-quantity/high-discount transactions, the result can look very different from what stakeholders expect when they think "average discount per unit."

dax: Revenue per Category = DIVIDE ( SUM ( Sales[SalesAmount] ), DISTINCTCOUNT ( Product[Category] ) )
explanation: This measure calculates the average revenue spread across product categories - essentially, how much sales revenue each distinct category contributes on average. It sums all SalesAmount values in the current filter context, then divides that by the count of unique Category values visible in that same context, using DIVIDE to safely return blank instead of an error when the denominator is zero. The key watch-out is that DISTINCTCOUNT operates on the Product table's Category column, so if your model has an inactive or broken relationship between Sales and Product, the category count may reflect all categories in the Product table rather than only those with actual sales - inflating the denominator and understating the per-category figure.
